# Financial Deep Research: L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited (GLS)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07
Stage 1 baseline provided: No — §9 notes the absence and is deferred.

## 1. Recent Financial Performance & Quarterly Trends

**Metric Selection Note:** GLS is a listed investment company (LIC) implementing a global long-short equity strategy. Traditional operating metrics (revenue, EBITDA, operating cash flow) are not meaningful lenses. The appropriate financial performance indicators are net tangible asset (NTA) per share movement, portfolio net returns versus benchmark, fee-adjusted returns, the premium or discount to NTA, and portfolio leverage/exposure.

### Most Recent Report
The most recent formal financial report is the **Half-Year Report for the six months ended 31 December 2025**, released on 23 February 2026 [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: GLS Half-Year Report 31 December 2025 / Date: 2026-02-23]***. The most recent continuous disclosure items are the **Monthly Net Tangible Asset Backing as at 30 April 2026** (announced 4 May 2026) and the **Weekly Net Tangible Asset Backing as at 6 May 2026** (announced 7 May 2026) [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Monthly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-04]***; [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Weekly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07]***.

### Most Recent Full Year Report
The entity was formerly Platinum Capital Limited (PMC). The **2025 Annual Report** (for the year ended 30 June 2025) was released under the PMC name on 25 August 2025 [Source: Company 10-K equivalent / Title: PMC 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2025-08-25]***. It reflects the old strategy and is of limited relevance to the current L1-managed portfolio.

### Key Business Drivers in Recent Quarters
The transition to L1 Capital occurred on **28 November 2025**. Since then, portfolio performance has been highly volatile:

- **January 2026:** +5.8% net return [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: January 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-02-11]***
- **February 2026:** +3.3% net return [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: February 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-11]***
- **March 2026:** -14.9% net return (MSCI World -6.4%) [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***
- **April 2026:** -7.0% net return (MSCI World -3.6% for the quarter; monthly MSCI not separately cited in source) [Source: Company Quarterly Report / Title: Quarterly Report - March 2026 / Date: 2026-04-21]***

The March/April drawdown was driven by the **Iran War**, which triggered a spike in volatility, a ~50% rise in Brent crude, and a sharp global equity selloff in which 84% of S&P 500 stocks fell [Source: Company Investor Presentation / Title: GLS Investor Presentation / Date: 2026-04-21]***. The manager used the volatility to add to gold, construction materials, European banks, and copper, while trimming energy and infrastructure names [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***.

**NTA per share progression (post-tax):**
- 31 December 2025: **$1.6540** [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***
- 31 March 2026: **$1.55** [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***
- 30 April 2026: **$1.6404** [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Monthly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-04]***
- 6 May 2026: **$1.6314** [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Weekly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07]***

The share price has traded at a **premium to NTA** throughout the period. At 6 May 2026, with the share price at approximately **$1.87** [Source: StockAnalysis / Title: GLS Stock Price & Overview / Date: 2026-05-07]**, the stock commands a **~14.6% premium to post-tax NTA** and a **~18.5% premium to pre-tax NTA** ($1.5778) [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Weekly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07]***.

### Segment Performance
As a single-strategy fund, segment reporting is not provided in the statutory accounts. However, portfolio attribution commentary indicates:
- **H1 2026 (Nov–Dec 2025) positive contributors:** IT, Communication Services, and Healthcare. Key names mentioned include Alphabet, Micron Technology, and TSMC [Source: ShareCafe / Title: "L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results" / Date: 2026-02-23]*.
- **Q1 2026 negative detractors:** Gold (price fell ~12% from ~$5,280 to ~$4,670), Cellnex, and HDFC Bank [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***.

### Geographic & FX Exposure
Gross market-cap exposure as at 31 March 2026 was:
- North America: **37%**
- Asia-Pacific: **34%**
- Europe: **19%**
- Other (global hedges): **11%** [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***

Net regional exposure was heavily skewed to Europe (+55%) and Asia-Pacific (+39%), while North America was +41% net and Other was -36% (likely hedges) [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***. No explicit currency hedging data is disclosed in the public extracts reviewed.

---

## 2. Growth Trajectory

### Trajectory Assessment
For an actively managed LIC, "growth" is measured by **NAV/NTA per-share compounding and portfolio net returns**, not revenue growth. The look-back window is constrained by the strategy change. Two windows are relevant:

1. **The unlisted L1 Global Long Short Strategy** (inception 1 January 2025): This generated a **+69.6% net return in calendar 2025** and **+67.5% YTD to 30 November 2025** [Source: Company Investor Presentation / Title: GLS Investor Presentation (Dec 2025) / Date: 2025-12-02]***.
2. **The listed GLS vehicle** (inception 28 November 2025): Post-transition performance has been negative in Q1 2026. The strategy delivered strong absolute returns in its first 11 months of existence but has since suffered a sharp drawdown.

The trajectory appears **front-loaded and volatile**. The 2025 returns were exceptional, but the Q1 2026 correction wiped out a material portion of the post-transition gains. The manager claims a long-term net return target of **10% p.a.** [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08]**.

### Peer-Relative Growth Context
- **Since strategy inception (Jan 2025):** The fund reports **53.9% net return p.a.**, compared to the MSCI World Accumulation Index at **13.2% p.a.** [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS Investor Presentation Summary (April 2026) / Date: 2026-04-30]*.
- **Q1 2026:** GLS returned **-7.0%** vs MSCI World **-3.6%**, representing **underperformance of 340 bps** in a down quarter [Source: Company Quarterly Report / Title: Quarterly Report - March 2026 / Date: 2026-04-21]***.
- **March 2026 alone:** GLS **-14.9%** vs MSCI World **-6.4%**, underperforming by 850 bps [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***.

The peer-relative picture has shifted dramatically: from substantial outperformance in 2025 to meaningful underperformance in the first quarter of 2026. The manager attributes the Q1 underperformance to long-biased exposure (net ~99–100%) into a sharp market correction and specific long-position drawdowns.

---

## 3. Margin & Profitability Profile

**Metric Selection Note:** For an investment manager vehicle, "profitability" is best assessed through **fee drag, cost-to-NAV ratio, and the spread between gross and net returns**. Operating margins are irrelevant because the company is a pass-through investment vehicle.

### Fee Structure & Cost Drag
- **Management fee:** **1.44% p.a.** of portfolio NAV (inclusive of GST, net of RITC) [Source: Company Fact Sheet / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited (ASX:GLS) / Date: 2026-03-31]***.
- **Performance fee:** **20.5% p.a.** of any increase in NAV over each 6-month performance period, subject to a **high watermark** [Source: Company Fact Sheet / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited (ASX:GLS) / Date: 2026-03-31]***.
- **Fee holiday:** **No management fee** for the 12 months from 28 November 2025 [Source: Company Announcement / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Update / Date: 2026-01-31]***. Performance fees still apply during this period.

### Peer-Relative Profitability Context
The fee load is **above average** for Australian LICs. The pre-transition Platinum strategy charged 1.1% management and 15% performance (subject to benchmark outperformance recovery), whereas the L1 structure charges 1.4% and 20% with no benchmark hurdle (absolute return high watermark only) [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***. The fee structure is therefore **more aggressive** and shifts more value to the manager in volatile markets. The current management-fee waiver masks this cost for the first year.

---

## 4. Cash Generation & Quality

**Metric Selection Note:** For a long-short fund, "cash generation" is not operating cash flow but rather **realised trading gains, dividend income from long positions, and the sustainability of distributions**. FCF is not a meaningful metric here.

### Cash Generation Quality Assessment
The fund generates cash through:
1. **Realised and unrealised gains/losses** on long and short equity positions (the primary driver).
2. **Dividend and interest income** from long positions and cash deposits.
3. **Securities lending revenue** (permitted under the new IMA) [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***.

**H1 2026 (half-year to 31 Dec 2025):**
- Total revenue and other income: **$91.8m** (up ~500% YoY, reflecting the new strategy and capital raise) [Source: ShareCafe / Title: "L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results" / Date: 2026-02-23]*.
- Profit after tax: **$57.5m** [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.
- Basic EPS: **20.96 cents** [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.

The earnings quality is **highly volatile by design**; they are mark-to-market driven. The H1 profit was overwhelmingly unrealisation-dependent.

### Working Capital & Cash Conversion
Working capital dynamics are **not material** in the traditional sense. The fund holds:
- **Cash at bank:** $11.6m (31 Dec 2025) [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.
- **Cash on deposit held within the portfolio:** $458.3m [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.
- **Receivables:** $77.6m (largely settlements and dividends receivable) [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.

This provides short-term liquidity, but the primary liquidity mechanism is the **prime broker arrangement**, which finances the leveraged long book and short book.

### Earnings Quality
Earnings are **predominantly unrealised**. The Statement of Financial Position shows:
- Financial assets at FVTPL: **$1,248.3m**
- Financial liabilities at FVTPL: **$602.4m** [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.

Deferred tax liabilities are modest ($4.2m), but income tax payable of $21.8m suggests realised gains were crystallised during the portfolio transition and early deployment [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***. The existence of a **Dividend Profit Reserve** of **$167.5m** (as at 31 Dec 2025) indicates a formal ring-fencing of distributable profits, which supports dividend sustainability [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.

---

## 5. Balance Sheet & Liquidity

### Leverage, Exposure & Refinancing Risk
GLS does not carry traditional term debt on its balance sheet. Instead, leverage is obtained via **prime broker arrangements** and the reinvestment of short-sale proceeds.

**Exposure as at 31 March 2026:**
- **Gross long exposure:** 217% of NAV
- **Gross short exposure:** 118% of NAV
- **Net exposure:** 99% of NAV [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***

**IMA limits:**
- Maximum net exposure: **150%** of NAV
- Maximum gross exposure: **350%** of NAV
- Typical gross expected: **150–300%** of NAV [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***.

The fund is operating at the **upper end** of its typical gross exposure range (217% long + 118% short = 335% gross), leaving limited headroom before hitting the 350% cap.

**Balance sheet snapshot (31 December 2025):**
| Item | $m |
|---|---|
| Total assets | 1,784.9 |
| Financial assets (longs) | 1,248.3 |
| Cash on deposit | 458.3 |
| Receivables | 77.6 |
| **Total liabilities** | **883.1** |
| Financial liabilities at FVTPL (shorts) | 602.4 |
| Payables | 254.7 |
| Income tax payable | 21.8 |
| Deferred tax liabilities | 4.2 |
| **Net assets / Equity** | **901.8** |
| Issued capital | 809.4 |
| Accumulated losses | (75.1) |
| Dividend profit reserve | 167.5 |

[Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***

### Assessment
Liquidity is **adequate but concentrated in prime broker relationships**. The $458m cash deposit provides a buffer, but the fund relies on prime brokers for:
- Financing long positions,
- Securities borrowing for shorts,
- Valuation and custody,
- Rehypothecation of assets [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***.

**Prime broker concentration risk** is material: if a prime broker fails or changes margin terms, the fund could face forced deleveraging. The only disclosed debt leverage providers are the prime brokers [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***.

There is **no term debt maturity profile** to analyse. The relevant refinancing risk is the **rollover of prime broker agreements and margin terms**.

---

## 6. Capital Deployment & Returns on Capital

### Share Repurchases
No active buyback program is in place post-transition. A **pre-transition buyback** of 43.9m shares occurred between August and December 2025 as part of the scheme of arrangement [Source: Company Half-Year Report / Title: H1 2026 Results / Date: 2026-02-23]***.

### Dividends
- **Interim dividend:** 1 cent per share, fully franked, paid 20 March 2026 (ex-date 5 March 2026) [Source: ShareCafe / Title: "L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results" / Date: 2026-02-23]*.
- **Dividend Reinvestment Plan (DRP):** Operational; shares acquired through DRP are "usually at a discount to the market price" per company disclosure [Source: L1 Capital Website / Title: GLS Dividend Reinvestment Plan / Date: 2026-04-28]*. The interim dividend had **no discount** applied [Source: ShareCafe / Title: "L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results" / Date: 2026-02-23]*.
- **Policy:** The Board intends to provide a **"sustainable and growing stream of fully franked dividends"** [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08]*.

Historical dividends under the old PMC regime were 3c semi-annual. The new 1c interim suggests a **step-down or reset** consistent with the transition.

### M&A / Capex
Not applicable. Capital is deployed into the investment portfolio.

### Returns on Capital
The appropriate measure is **return on NTA**:
- **H1 2026:** Pre-tax NTA rose from $1.5151 to $1.6540 per share. Total shareholder return was **29.2%** for the half-year, driven partly by portfolio returns and partly by a narrowing of the share-price discount to NTA [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08]*.
- **Q1 2026 (calendar):** Pre-tax NTA fell from ~$1.70 (implied from Feb data) to $1.48 by 31 March 2026, before recovering to $1.5947 by 30 April 2026 [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***; [Source: ASX Announcement / Title: Monthly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-04]***.

The fund is currently trading at a **premium to NTA**, implying the market is pricing in **future manager alpha**. If the fund were to revert to a discount (as it did persistently under Platinum), shareholder returns would significantly underperform NTA returns.

---

## 7. Management Guidance & Forward Outlook

### Most Recent Full Year Report Guidance
No formal FY2026 guidance was provided in the 2025 Annual Report (pre-transition). The new manager does not provide short-term earnings guidance.

### Most Recent Report Guidance
The Board and manager have provided the following forward-facing statements:
- **Long-term return target:** **10% p.a. net** [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08]*.
- **Dividend intention:** Sustainable and growing fully franked dividend stream [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08]*.
- **Portfolio positioning (April 2026):** Manager believes the portfolio looks "particularly attractive" with the median long position on ~8x P/E, double-digit EPS growth, and modest debt [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***.

### Management Commentary
In the March 2026 monthly report, L1 Capital noted:
> "The Iran War has triggered a widespread fall in equity markets, with a major spike in market volatility. We are using this period of elevated volatility to identify high quality companies that are now trading far below fair value, even assuming a less favourable macro outlook." [Source: Company Monthly Report / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-04-12]***

In the April 2026 investor presentation, the firm highlighted its track record of capitalising on volatile periods and noted **broad-based returns across 51 stocks** since inception [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS Investor Presentation Summary (April 2026) / Date: 2026-04-30]*.

### Forward Claim Confidence Weighting
| Forward Claim | Confidence | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **10% p.a. long-term net return** | **Low-Medium** | Track record since Jan 2025 is strong (+69.6% in 2025), but the strategy is less than 18 months old. Q1 2026 drawdown of -14.9% in one month demonstrates high volatility. |
| **Sustainable and growing dividend** | **Medium** | Supported by $167.5m Dividend Profit Reserve (Dec 2025), but dividend capacity depends on realised gains and franking credit balances. No franking account balance is disclosed. |
| **Continued premium to NTA** | **Low** | The premium is a recent phenomenon (post-transition). Historically, PMC traded at a persistent discount. If performance falters, the premium could reverse quickly. |

---

## 8. Material Financial Risks & One-Off Items

### The Iran War Market Shock (Material One-Off / Ongoing)
The March/April 2026 selloff is the defining financial event of the recent period. It caused:
- A **-14.9% single-month drawdown** in March.
- A **-7.0% quarterly return** vs -3.6% for MSCI World.
- A spike in volatility and central bank rate-hike repricing [Source: Company Investor Presentation / Title: GLS Investor Presentation / Date: 2026-04-21]***.
- Collateral pressure on leveraged long/short books.

This event is **ongoing as of the research date**; its full financial impact on Q2 2026 results is not yet crystallised.

### Prime Broker & Counterparty Risk
The fund relies on prime brokers for custody, execution, valuation, securities lending, and leverage. Assets may be **rehypothecated** by prime brokers. Failure or distress of a prime broker could freeze assets or force rapid deleveraging [Source: Platinum Capital / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29]***.

### Leverage Amplification Risk
With gross exposure at **335%** (217% long + 118% short) and net exposure near **100%**, the portfolio is highly leveraged. A 10% move in underlying equities can drive ~20%+ moves in NAV. The March 2026 experience confirms this.

### Short Selling Risk
Short positions have theoretically unlimited loss potential and create collateral requirements. In a short squeeze or rapid rally, the fund could face forced covering.

### Foreign Currency Risk
The fund invests globally but reports in AUD. FX swings impact NTA. No explicit hedging policy data was found in the reviewed disclosures.

### Key Person Risk
The strategy is tightly linked to Co-CIOs **Raphael Lamm** and **Mark Landau**. Their departure would likely impair the strategy and the premium valuation.

### Fee Structure Risk
When the 12-month management-fee waiver expires (November 2026), the headline cost drag will increase by **1.44% p.a.**, all else equal.

---

## 9. Discrepancies vs. Structured Data Baseline

### Research Data vs. Stage 1 Baseline
**Stage 1 baseline not provided at runtime — section deferred.** No structured baseline was supplied against which to reconcile findings.

---

## 10. Key Financial Assumptions Embedded in Current Valuation

The current share price of ~$1.87 (premium of ~14.6% to post-tax NTA) embeds the following load-bearing assumptions:

1. **Manager alpha sustainability:** The market assumes L1 Capital will continue to generate returns well above the MSCI World index. The 10% p.a. long-term target is priced as achievable, if not conservative.
2. **Premium permanence:** The stock trades at a premium, whereas the predecessor (PMC) traded at a persistent discount. The market assumes the new strategy justifies a premium or at least no discount.
3. **Drawdown recovery:** The March/April correction is assumed to be temporary, and the manager's positioning (8x P/E median longs) is assumed to drive a sharp rebound.
4. **Dividend growth:** A "sustainable and growing" dividend stream is priced in, implying the 1c interim will grow to a higher run-rate.
5. **Leverage discipline:** The market assumes gross exposure will not be forced down by margin calls or prime broker actions.
6. **Fee holiday extension or indifference:** The market appears indifferent to the impending expiry of the 12-month management-fee waiver in November 2026.

If any of these assumptions fail, the premium to NTA is at risk of compression or reversal.

---

## 11. Bottom-Line Financial Assessment

### Business Model Characterization
GLS is a **high-conviction, leveraged global long-short equity listed investment company**. It is an **absolute-return vehicle** with a value-biased, bottom-up stock selection process. The financial reality is one of **high volatility, high fee drag, and high sensitivity to manager skill and prime broker stability**. The appropriate analytical lenses are NTA compounding, premium/discount dynamics, leverage-adjusted downside capture, and fee-adjusted alpha generation.

### Strengths (confidence weighted)
- **Strong 2025 track record (Medium confidence):** The strategy returned +69.6% net in 2025 and outperformed benchmarks significantly. This is empirical evidence of manager skill in the specific market environment of 2025.
- **Experienced investment team (High confidence):** L1 Capital is a well-established manager with a long track record across other strategies. The founders remain majority owners.
- **Liquid balance sheet with cash buffer (Medium confidence):** $458m in portfolio cash deposits and $11.6m cash at bank (Dec 2025) provides operational liquidity.
- **Dividend profit reserve (Medium confidence):** The $167.5m reserve formalises distributable capacity.
- **Fee holiday (High confidence):** No management fee for 12 months from November 2025 improves net returns in the short term.

### Weaknesses (confidence weighted)
- **Extreme Q1 2026 volatility and underperformance (High confidence):** A -14.9% month and -7.0% quarter with 340-850 bps underperformance to MSCI World raises questions about downside protection.
- **High fee load post-waiver (High confidence):** 1.44% management + 20.5% performance fee (no benchmark hurdle, only high watermark) is expensive and favours the manager in volatile markets.
- **Premium to NTA vulnerability (High confidence):** The ~15% premium is not structurally anchored. A return to discount would destroy shareholder value even if NTA is flat.
- **High leverage (High confidence):** Gross exposure of 335% amplifies losses. The March drawdown demonstrated this vividly.
- **Prime broker dependence (Medium confidence):** Single-source financing and rehypothecation risk create tail-risk scenarios.
- **Short track record under current structure (High confidence):** The listed vehicle has only ~5 months of history. The 2025 unlisted track record may not be representative of the listed vehicle's future.

### Watch Items
1. **May 2026 monthly report:** Will NTA stabilise or continue declining? The weekly NTA to 6 May shows a slight downtick ($1.6404 to $1.6314).
2. **Premium/discount trajectory:** Any sustained widening of the discount would signal loss of market confidence.
3. **Gross exposure levels:** If the manager increases gross exposure to chase recovery, downside risk rises further.
4. **Prime broker disclosures:** Any change in prime broker relationships or margin terms.
5. **Fee waiver expiry (Nov 2026):** Impact on reported net returns and NTA growth.
6. **Franking account balance:** Critical for the sustainability of "fully franked" dividend claims. No data found.

---

## 12. Data Gaps & Limitations

| Missing Data Point | Search Scope Attempted | Analytical Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact sector weights as of April/May 2026** | Searched ASX announcements, monthly reports, investor presentation summaries | Precludes precise sector risk assessment; only anecdotal manager commentary available. |
| **Top 10 holdings / position concentration** | Searched monthly reports, investor presentation, fact sheets | Cannot assess single-name risk or concentration. The fund holds 102 positions (67 long, 35 short), but top 10 weights are unknown. |
| **Prime broker borrowings / margin loan principal** | Searched half-year report notes, AGM explanatory memorandum | The $602.4m financial liabilities are aggregate short positions and derivatives; the exact prime broker financing quantum is not split out. |
| **Interest expense on leverage** | Searched half-year report statement of profit or loss | Not separately disclosed; likely embedded in "transaction costs" or "other expenses." Precludes calculation of true cost of leverage. |
| **Performance fee accrual as at 31 Dec 2025 or 30 Apr 2026** | Searched half-year report, NTA announcements | Unknown whether a performance fee was crystallised at H1. This affects true NAV and distributable reserves. |
| **Franking credit balance / franking account** | Searched half-year report, dividend announcements | Sustainability of "fully franked" dividends cannot be verified beyond the current period. |
| **Final dividend amount for FY2026** | Searched ASX announcements, dividend forecasts | No guidance found. The "sustainable and growing" claim is aspirational until quantified. |
| **Detailed attribution for Jan–Apr 2026 (long vs short contribution)** | Searched monthly reports, quarterly report | Only anecdotal detractor/ contributor names provided; no formal attribution table found. |
| **Currency hedging policy and notional hedges** | Searched IMA terms, monthly reports | FX exposure is unknown; geographic exposure does not equal currency exposure. |
| **Stage 1 structured baseline** | N/A — not supplied at runtime | §9 deferred; no reconciliation possible. |

**Other Limitations:**
- The entity has **only ~5 months of listed history under the new strategy**, making statistical analysis of returns unreliable.
- The **March 2026 "Iran War"** is a recent, unmodelled geopolitical shock. Its persistence and second-order effects (energy prices, inflation, central bank policy) remain highly uncertain as of the research date.
- Sources conflict slightly on share count (547.2m at Dec 2025 vs 547.6m at March 2026); the difference likely reflects DRP issuance and is immaterial to the analysis.

# News & Narrative Deep Research: L1 Global Long Short Fund (GLS)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07

## 1. Most Significant News in the Window

The dominant narrative driver for GLS in the 90-day window is the collision between its high-octane strategy transition and a live geopolitical stress-test.

**Iran War and March 2026 Portfolio Carnage.** On March 31, 2026, GLS released its monthly report disclosing a **-14.9% net return for March** against an MSCI World decline of -6.4%. The fund described the period as triggered by "the war in Iran," which caused "the worst oil supply shock in history" via the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting roughly 20% of global oil flows and spiking Brent crude ~50%. The S&P 500 fell 5.0% and 84% of its constituents declined. Key portfolio detractors were long positions in gold (which fell ~12% as Turkey’s central bank dumped reserves), Cellnex, and HDFC Bank. For the full March quarter, the fund returned -7.0% (MSCI World -3.6%). [Source: ASX Research / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Monthly Report March 2026 / Date: 2026-03-31] ***

**Share Price Meltdown on Weekly NTA Release.** GLS sold off 9.5% on March 11, 2026, the day it released both the positive February monthly report (+3.3%) and a weekly NTA that reflected early March weakness as the Iran conflict escalated. This was the single largest one-day move in the window and signaled that the market was forward-looking through the rear-view mirror of February performance. [Source: Market Index / Title: Evening Wrap: ASX 200 logs second gain / Date: 2026-03-11] *

**Quarterly Report and Investor Presentation (April 21, 2026).** The company released its Quarterly Report for March 2026 and an accompanying Investor Presentation on April 21, 2026. Management framed the March drawdown against the strategy’s track record, noting the Global Long Short Fund had delivered 53.9% p.a. since inception (January 2025) and highlighting a "track-record of capitalising on periods of elevated market volatility." The presentation emphasized that 51 stocks had each contributed more than 1.0% to returns since inception. [Source: Listcorp / Title: GLS Investor Presentation / Date: 2026-04-20] **; [Source: Listcorp / Title: Quarterly Report - March 2026 / Date: 2026-04-21] **

**H1 2026 Financial Results.** On February 23, 2026, GLS reported half-year results to December 31, 2025. Profit after tax jumped to **$57.5m** (up 609% on the prior corresponding period) and pre-tax NTA per share rose from $1.515 to $1.654. Total shareholder return for the half was 29.2%, flattered by the narrowing discount-to-NTA during the transition. The Board declared a fully franked interim dividend of **1 cent per share**, payable March 20, 2026. [Source: Sharecafe / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results and Interim Dividend / Date: 2026-02-23] *

**Persistent Premium to NTA.** Despite the March drawdown, GLS has continued to trade at a material premium to net tangible assets. Weekly NTA as at May 1, 2026 was $1.5778 pre-tax and $1.6314 after-tax. With the share price trading at $1.87–$1.96 in early May, the stock carries a **~15% premium to after-tax NTA**. This is a sharp reversal from its historical average as Platinum Capital, which traded at an average discount of -8.7% over three years. [Source: ASX Announcement via Listcorp / Title: Weekly Net Tangible Asset Backing / Date: 2026-05-07] ***; [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: LIC Update - February 2026 / Date: 2026-03-17] *

No earnings call transcript or live management Q&A for GLS was identified in the research window. As a listed investment company, GLS does not appear to hold quarterly earnings calls akin to operating companies. The H1 2026 results were released via ASX announcement and press coverage on February 23, 2026, and the March quarterly update was delivered through written ASX filings and an investor presentation on April 21, 2026. [Source: Search scope: ASX announcements, L1 Capital investor relations, Quartr earnings database; no call transcript found.]

Absent a live call, management tone must be inferred from written commentary. The March 2026 monthly report struck a candid but resilient note. The fund acknowledged that "after a solid start to the year, portfolio performance was impacted by the fallout from the conflict in Iran," and itemized specific detractors (gold, Cellnex, HDFC Bank). At the same time, it contextualized losses against the MSCI World benchmark and reiterated the strategy’s 53.9% p.a. inception return. The April 2026 Investor Presentation leaned heavily into this long-term framing, asserting that the strategy "has a track record of capitalizing on volatile periods." [Source: ASX Research / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Monthly Report March 2026 / Date: 2026-03-31] ***; [Source: Listcorp / Title: GLS Investor Presentation / Date: 2026-04-20] **

However, the 9.5% single-day share price drop on March 11, 2026 suggests the market pushed back aggressively on the disconnect between February’s +3.3% monthly gain and the rapidly deteriorating weekly NTA as the Iran crisis unfolded. The company has not issued formal earnings guidance. The H1 2026 results stated the Board intends to provide a "sustainable and growing stream of fully franked dividends," starting from the 1c interim. [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Title: LIC Update - February 2026 / Date: 2026-03-17] *

No upgrades, downgrades, coverage initiations, or price target changes from named sell-side brokers were identified in the 90-day window. No analyst price targets were reliably sourced.

No material regulatory, legal, or compliance developments were identified in the research window. The fund operates as a standard Australian listed investment company regulated by ASIC and the ASX. Historical governance friction from 2025 (WAM Capital’s competing proposal and unsuccessful director nominations) was resolved before the window and is not an active narrative driver. [Source: Platinum Capital Limited / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29] **

### M&A Activity
No M&A activity in the window. The fund completed its transition from Platinum Capital Limited to L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited on November 28, 2025, including a portfolio monetization and reinvestment into the L1 Global Long Short Strategy.

### Leadership Changes

### Restructuring & Workforce Actions

### Capital Return Announcements

The DRP was in operation with no discount. The dividend represented a modest 2.2% trailing yield and a grossed-up yield of 3.1%. The market read the initiation of dividends as a normalization gesture rather than a strong income signal. [Source: Sharecafe / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Announces Half-Year Financial Results / Date: 2026-02-23] *

- **Katrina Glendinning**: bought 9,934 shares at ~$2.00 on February 25, 2026, and 25,900 shares at ~$1.92 on February 24, 2026.

These purchases totaled roughly $97,000 and were the only material insider transactions in the window. [Source: Motley Fool / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Ltd Share Price News / Date: 2026-05-06] **

## 6. Sentiment & Positioning

### Institutional Sentiment
Institutional support was cemented during the December 2025 capital raising. L1 Group and its founders committed ~$90 million, while external cornerstone partners added ~$72 million, for a combined ~$162 million in cornerstone backing. The 1-for-1 entitlement offer raised up to $415 million at $1.63 per share. No substantial holder notices were filed in the 90-day window, suggesting no major institutional exits or initiations in the immediate period. [Source: Quartr / Title: L1 Global Long Short Fund Investor Presentation Summary / Date: 2025-12-02] **

HotCopper forum threads for GLS announcements in the window received zero comments, and no Reddit, Twitter, or StockTwits activity was identified at a level affecting the stock. This is marked explicitly as lower-signal noise. [Source: HotCopper / Title: GLS Discussion Threads / Date: 2026-05-06]

### Short Interest
No short interest data was found via public search. Search scope: ASX short-selling disclosures, Market Index, and financial data aggregators.

### Valuation Narrative
The market is actively debating GLS’s valuation premium. As a LIC, GLS historically traded at a discount to NTA (average -8.7% over three years as Platinum Capital). Since the transition to L1 Capital, it has swung to a **persistent premium**—peaking at an estimated +19.6% to marked-down NTA in mid-March 2026 and settling around +15% to after-tax NTA by early May. The bull case holds that the premium is justified by L1 Capital’s 53.9% p.a. track record, the 12-month fee holiday, and scarcity value of a global long/short LIC wrapper. The bear case argues the premium is fragile: it prices in unlisted fund returns inside a taxed, locked-in LIC structure with a 1.10% MER and no redemption rights, and it assumes the Iran-war drawdown is temporary rather than a sign of strategy capacity constraints.

## 7. Short-Seller Research & Activist Investor Involvement

No short-seller reports or activist activity were identified in the research window. WAM Capital’s 2025 campaign to install alternative directors was defeated in October/November 2025 and is no longer an active overhang. [Source: Platinum Capital Limited / Title: Notice of Annual General Meeting 2025 / Date: 2025-10-29] **

## 8. Narrative Shifts

**Prior narrative (December 2025–February 2026):** GLS was a "transition winner"—a successful manager change from a struggling value manager to a high-performance global long/short strategy, supported by a $415 million equity raise, no management fee for 12 months, and a 67.5% unlisted fund track record.

**Current narrative (April–May 2026):** GLS is now a "stress-tested premium LIC." The Iran war and the -14.9% March month forced the market to confront whether the strategy’s volatility edge translates inside a listed wrapper. The dominant story is the tension between the strategy’s strong long-term returns and the stock’s anomalous premium to NTA after a brutal quarter.

**Trigger:** The release of the March 2026 monthly report on March 31, 2026, quantifying the Iran-war damage and the fund’s underperformance versus its own track record, followed by the April 21 quarterly report confirming the -7.0% quarter.

## 9. Narrative-vs-Reality Disconnects

A **clear, material disconnect** exists: GLS trades at roughly a **15% premium to after-tax NTA** despite delivering a -7.0% quarter and a -14.9% month in March 2026. This premium appears to price the unlisted strategy’s 53.9% p.a. inception return directly into the LIC share price, ignoring the structural drag of corporate tax, the 1.10% MER, and the lack of redemption rights. 

Additionally, the H1 FY26 profit of $57.5m and 29.2% shareholder return were inflated by one-off portfolio monetization effects and the equity raise, not by ongoing investment performance. The market’s willingness to award a premium on the basis of pre-transition track record, despite a live drawdown, creates a fragile valuation foundation.

## 10. Bottom-Line Narrative Assessment

### Narrative Characterization
GLS is currently perceived as a high-conviction, high-volatility vehicle for accessing L1 Capital’s global long/short strategy. The market has awarded it a rare and persistent LIC premium based on historical returns, but that premium is now being stress-tested by geopolitical volatility and a visible strategy drawdown. The dominant story is no longer "transition and inflows" but "can the track record justify the premium?"

### Key Narrative Drivers (confidence weighted)

1. **Iran war impact and March 2026 drawdown** — *High confidence.* The -14.9% month is the single most discussed event; it reframed the fund from a momentum story to a volatility story. [Source: ASX Research / Monthly Report March 2026 / Date: 2026-03-31] ***
2. **Persistent premium to NTA (~15%)** — *High confidence.* Documented by weekly NTA announcements and Lowell Resources Fund data. This is the key valuation debate. [Source: ASX Announcement / Weekly NTA / Date: 2026-05-07] ***; [Source: Lowell Resources Fund / Date: 2026-03-17] *
3. **L1 Capital’s 53.9% p.a. track record since Jan 2025** — *High confidence.* Management cites this in every presentation; it is the load-bearing justification for the premium. [Source: Listcorp / GLS Investor Presentation / Date: 2026-04-20] **
4. **12-month management fee holiday** — *Medium confidence.* Expires November 28, 2026. It was a key selling point of the transition but is now halfway consumed. [Source: Quartr / Investor Presentation Summary / Date: 2025-12-02] **
5. **Director buying in February 2026** — *Medium confidence.* Small in dollar terms (~$97k) but symbolically noted as alignment. [Source: Motley Fool / Director Transactions / Date: 2026-05-06] **

### Narrative Risks (confidence weighted)

1. **Premium compression if volatility persists** — *High confidence.* If the fund delivers another down month or the Iran conflict escalates further, the ~15% premium could revert rapidly toward historical discount levels (-8.7% 3-year average), implying significant share price downside.
2. **Failure to replicate unlisted fund returns inside LIC wrapper** — *Medium confidence.* Tax drag, cash drag from the DRP/dividend policy, and corporate costs may structurally underperform the reference strategy.
3. **End of fee holiday and performance fee crystallization** — *Medium confidence.* From November 28, 2026, management fees resume and performance fees (if any) will become more visible, potentially pressuring NTA growth.
4. **Geopolitical risk in the portfolio** — *Medium confidence.* The March report revealed unexpected sensitivity to gold and Indian financials (HDFC Bank) during an oil shock, suggesting short-portfolio hedges may not be as effective as marketed.

### Catalysts to Watch

- **Weekly NTA announcements** (every Wednesday/Thursday; next expected ~May 13, 2026)
- **Monthly report for April 2026** (expected mid-May 2026)
- **Q2 2026 Quarterly Report** (expected late July 2026)
- **FY2026 Annual Results** (expected late August 2026)
- **Expiry of 12-month no-management-fee period** (November 28, 2026)
- **Next dividend declaration** (likely August 2026, based on historical LIC reporting cycle)

## 11. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **No earnings call transcript or live management Q&A** was found for the research window. Search scope: ASX announcements, L1 Capital IR page, Quartr database. Analytical impact: tone and conviction assessments rely on written filings rather than direct Q&A.

# Competitive & Macro Deep Research: L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited (GLS)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07

## 1. Competitive Position & Market Share

### Peer Set & Justification
GLS is an ASX-listed Listed Investment Company (LIC) offering a leveraged, absolute-return global long-short equity strategy. Two peer sets are relevant. The **primary peer set** comprises other ASX-listed vehicles competing for Australian retail capital in the global equity and alternatives sleeve: MFF Capital Investments (MFF), WAM Global (WGB), PM Capital Global Opportunities (PGF), L1 Long Short Fund (LSF), and Regal Investment Fund (RF1). These are the right comparisons because they target similar investor segments—retail and advised clients seeking either global equity exposure or alternative risk-adjusted returns—and are evaluated on the same metrics of NTA performance, discount/premium to NTA, fee drag, and yield. The **secondary peer set** is unlisted global hedge funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs), which compete for the same investment objective but through different structures.

### Market Share & Position
As of February 2026, GLS carried a market capitalisation of approximately A$1.08 billion with 547.6 million shares on issue, placing it in the mid-tier of ASX-listed global equity vehicles [Source: Bell Potter/Firstlinks / Title: LIC Weekly Report Indicative NTA / Date: 2026-02-23] *. Among direct ASX peers, MFF Capital is larger at ~A$2.84 billion, PGF is ~A$1.52 billion, and WGB is ~A$828 million [Source: Bell Potter/Firstlinks / Title: LIC Weekly Report Indicative NTA / Date: 2026-02-23] *. In the alternatives category, LSF—managed by the same investment manager but with an Australian focus—has a market cap of ~A$2.50 billion [Source: Lowell Resources/Bell Potter / Title: LIC Update - March 2026 / Date: 2026-03-31] *. GLS is the only pure global long-short LIC at scale on the ASX, giving it a differentiated niche, though its asset base remains smaller than the long-only global giants and its domestic long-short sibling.

### Relative Positioning
GLS differentiates itself through an absolute-return mandate, the ability to run short positions, and higher leverage (maximum gross exposure 3.5x, net exposure up to 1.5x) versus long-only peers [Source: L1 Capital / Title: L1 Capital Global Long Short Strategy / Date: 2026-04-13] *. This positioning commands a higher fee: a 1.44% management fee and a 20.5% performance fee with a high watermark, compared with MFF’s ~0.19% indirect cost ratio and WGB’s 1.47% management fee [Source: ASX Research / Title: GLS Company Research File / Date: 2026-03-31] *; [Source: Bell Potter / Title: LIC September Quarter 2024 / Date: 2024-12-23] *. The strategy’s short track record as GLS (inception under L1 Capital on 28 November 2025) means it is still building a brand distinct from L1’s flagship Australian long-short strategy, which has topped peer rankings since 2014 [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: ASX Presentation / Date: 2025-07-08] ***.

## 2. Competitive Moat

### Moat Sources
GLS’s moat derives from three sources, all ultimately rooted in the investment manager rather than the corporate shell.

1. **Manager brand and investment process (intangible asset).** L1 Capital, founded in 2007 by co-CIOs Mark Landau and Raphael Lamm, has established a track record as a top-quartile Australian long-short manager. The flagship L1 Long Short Fund (LSF) is ranked #1 in its sector since inception (to May 2025) and preserved 85% of capital in ASX 200 down months through June 2025 [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: ASX Presentation / Date: 2025-07-08] ***. GLS leverages the same 22-person investment team and requires both co-CIOs to approve every new position, long or short [Source: L1 Capital / Title: L1 Capital Global Long Short Strategy / Date: 2026-04-13] *.
2. **Captive capital via the LIC structure (switching costs).** Unlike open-ended managed funds, GLS does not face daily redemptions. Shareholders must sell on-market, which insulates the portfolio from flow-driven liquidity events and gives the manager a stable capital base to run a concentrated, levered book.
3. **Scale economies in research.** As part of L1 Group—formed from the October 2025 merger of L1 Capital and Platinum Asset Management—the strategy benefits from a shared research platform and multi-channel distribution across seven national dealer groups and six research houses/asset consultants [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: ASX Presentation / Date: 2025-07-08] ***.

### Moat Durability Assessment
- **Manager brand:** *Strengthening near-term, but durability is conditional on performance persistence.* The GLS strategy itself has generated a 53.9% net annualised return since inception (1 January 2025 for the Daily Class) and a pre-tax NTA return of 9.2% in its first half as a transitioned LIC [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *; [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08] *. However, the fund’s history under L1 Global management spans less than six months, and the parent LSF track record, while strong, does not guarantee global long-short replication. Brand erosion could occur within 12–24 months if performance falters.
- **Captive capital:** *Stable but vulnerable to discount widening.* The LIC structure locks in capital, yet shareholders can still exit via price. The share price traded at a premium to NTA through March 2026 (share price A$1.62 vs pre-tax NTA A$1.48), but LIC premiums can reverse violently [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *. If the discount widens structurally, capital stability becomes a moot advantage.
- **Research scale:** *Strengthening.* L1 Group assets under management reached A$17.6 billion as of December 2025, with long-short strategies growing from A$5.6 billion to A$7.5 billion in a single quarter, driven partly by strong GLS inflows [Source: L1 Group / Title: ASX Announcement - AUM and Performance Fees Update / Date: 2026-01-13] ***. The platform is likely to remain stable barring key-person departures.

## 3. Competitive Threats

- **Passive and low-cost active vehicles (active threat).** MFF Capital charges roughly 0.19% and global equity ETFs charge less than 0.20%. GLS’s total cost structure (1.44% management fee plus a 20.5% performance fee) demands sustained alpha generation of several hundred basis points just to break even on fees versus beta alternatives [Source: ASX Research / Title: GLS Company Research File / Date: 2026-03-31] *; [Source: Bell Potter / Title: LIC September Quarter 2024 / Date: 2024-12-23] *. This threat is already compressing demand for traditional active global equity mandates.
- **Sibling cannibalisation (latent threat).** LSF offers the same manager, a similar process, and a longer track record in an Australian context with up to 30% global exposure. Investors seeking L1 long-short exposure may prefer LSF’s ~A$2.5 billion scale and established premium [Source: Lowell Resources/Bell Potter / Title: LIC Update - March 2026 / Date: 2026-03-31] *.
- **Key person risk (latent threat).** Landau and Lamm are co-CIOs, founders, and together with senior staff own 95% of L1 Capital [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: ASX Presentation / Date: 2025-07-08] ***. The investment process is explicitly dependent on their joint approval for new positions. Loss of either would likely trigger immediate outflows and a collapse in the strategy’s multiple.
- **New entrant competition (latent threat).** Wilson Asset Management and other well-distributed Australian managers have the retail footprint and balance sheet to launch competing global long-short LICs. Wilson’s existing equity-income vehicles already command premiums to NTA, demonstrating pricing power [Source: Morningstar / Title: Are LICs licked? / Date: 2025-09-18] **.

## 4. Industry Growth & Demand Outlook (1–2 Year Horizon)

### Industry Growth Trajectory
The Australian LIC/LIT sector is mature and consolidating. The number of listed vehicles fell from a peak of 115 in 2019 to 89 in 2025, yet aggregate market capitalisation grew approximately 12% year-on-year as capital concentrated in larger, differentiated funds [Source: Morningstar / Title: Are LICs licked? / Date: 2025-09-18] **. Global equity and fixed-income categories captured the majority of recent flows, while mid- and small-cap equity LICs trade at the widest discounts [Source: Morningstar / Title: Listed investment companies monthly performance: January 2026 / Date: 2026-02-20] **.

### Demand Drivers & Headwinds
**Drivers:** The March 2026 Iran War shock— which triggered an ~50% spike in Brent crude and a broad equity selloff—has increased demand for absolute-return and capital-preservation strategies [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *. GLS’s ability to run net exposure below 100% and profit from short positions is theoretically well-suited to this regime. **Headwinds:** Retail capital continues to favour yield-oriented vehicles. Equity-income LICs such as Plato Income Maximiser trade at 18% premiums to NTA, while global growth and traditional global equity LICs remain at or near their widest discounts in history [Source: Morningstar / Title: Are LICs licked? / Date: 2025-09-18] **.

### Industry Life Cycle & Structural Trends
The Australian LIC sector is a **mature, structurally challenged industry**. The shrinkage in vehicle count alongside ETF market-cap growth indicates a secular shift away from closed-ended active funds toward open-ended passive vehicles. For global equity LICs specifically, life-cycle dynamics are adverse: unless they deliver persistent alpha or unique income, they face ongoing discount pressure and eventual wind-up or conversion. GLS’s long-short structure partially insulates it from pure beta commoditisation, but it does not eliminate the structural headwind.

## 5. Customer & Supplier Concentration

### Customer Concentration
Revenue is derived from a diversified retail shareholder register accessed via the ASX. There is no evidence of material concentration among top shareholders. The company operates a Dividend Reinvestment Plan (DRP) to encourage retail retention [Source: L1 Capital / Title: GLS Investor Webpage / Date: 2026-04-28] *.

### Supplier Dependencies
Supplier concentration is **material**. The entire investment management function is outsourced to a single related party, L1 Capital Pty Ltd (investment manager), which is controlled by L1 Group Limited (ASX:L1G) [Source: L1 Group / Title: ASX Announcement - AUM and Performance Fees Update / Date: 2026-01-13] ***. GLS pays management fees (1.44%) and performance fees (20.5%) to L1 Capital, creating a locked-in related-party dependency [Source: ASX Research / Title: GLS Company Research File / Date: 2026-03-31] *. Custody and share registry services are outsourced to MUFG Corporate Markets, and prime brokerage for shorting is presumably spread across global banks, though specific prime broker identities are not disclosed [Source: L1 Capital / Title: GLS Investor Webpage / Date: 2026-04-28] *. The investment management relationship is the critical supplier risk.

## 6. Macroeconomic Sensitivities

### Standard Macro Variables
- **Interest rates:** Highly material. The fund runs maximum gross exposure of 3.5x and uses short selling, making it sensitive to the cost of carry and prime brokerage rates. The March 2026 Iran War prompted a rapid shift from expected rate cuts to hikes, with sovereign yields rising 35–70 basis points within weeks, increasing volatility and likely squeezing short-book carry [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *.
- **FX (AUD):** Material. Portfolio returns are reported in Australian dollars. AUD weakness against USD, EUR and HKD—the primary investment currencies—provides a tailwind to reported returns, while AUD strength creates a headwind.
- **Equity volatility and cross-asset correlation:** Highly material. Long-short alpha generation depends on stock-level dispersion. The Iran War caused a correlation spike in March 2026, with 84% of S&P 500 stocks declining simultaneously, compressing the efficacy of both long and short books [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *.
- **Inflation:** Material via second-order effects. The supply shock forced central banks to tighten into a slowing economy, increasing the probability of stagflation—a regime that is historically challenging for fundamental stock pickers.
- **Energy/commodity prices:** Material. Brent crude surged past $120 per barrel by March 2026 following the Strait of Hormuz closure, and GLS actively traded energy exposure, trimming outperforming energy names and adding to copper and gold [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *; [Source: Xinhua / Title: World Insights: Middle East war pushes up oil prices / Date: 2026-03-04] *.
- **Consumer spending / labour markets:** Immaterial for an investment fund with no direct operating leverage to the real economy.

### Company-Specific Macro Variables
- **Global equity market depth and short-selling liquidity:** Specifically material for GLS. The strategy requires liquid borrow pools for 35+ short positions and the ability to run 217% gross long exposure. A sustained tightening in short-selling regulation (e.g., SEC or HKEX restrictions) would directly constrain the strategy.
- **Australian retail risk appetite and LIC sector sentiment:** A uniquely Australian macro variable. The willingness of retail investors to hold closed-ended funds at a discount or premium drives GLS’s cost of capital and ability to grow via equity issuance. Current sector-wide discount dynamics are near historic extremes [Source: Morningstar / Title: Are LICs licked? / Date: 2025-09-18] **.

## 7. Regulatory Environment

GLS operates under a standard Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL 314302) held by L1 Capital Pty Ltd and is regulated by ASIC under the Corporations Act. The current regulatory backdrop is largely stable, with no active LIC-specific crackdown identified. ASIC’s 2025–26 corporate plan prioritises licensing efficiency and industry funding recovery rather than structural product intervention [Source: ASIC / Title: Corporate Plan 2025-26 / Date: 2025-08-26] ***. One pending matter is ASIC’s proposal to alter stamp-duty disclosure in fees-and-costs summaries, requiring averaging over seven years rather than annual disclosure [Source: ASIC / Title: Regulatory Tracker 2025 / Date: 2025-11-28] ***. While this is a disclosure tweak rather than a fee cap, it could marginally reduce fee transparency for investors comparing LICs.

Tax treatment remains favourable to the LIC structure: company tax is paid at 30%, dividends are fully franked, and capital gains can be retained and distributed with CGT concessions. No imminent legislative threat to franking credits or LIC tax status was identified in the search scope.

## 8. Technology & Structural Disruption Risks

### Identified Disruption Threats
1. **Passive ETF proliferation.** Global equity ETFs now offer diversified exposure for less than 20 basis points. GLS’s fee load requires persistent, high-alpha generation to justify the cost differential. This is not a future risk; it is actively compressing the addressable market for high-fee active global products today.
2. **AI-driven investment research.** The rise of quantamental strategies and large-language-model equity research could erode the inefficiencies that GLS’s bottom-up, contrarian, fundamental process exploits. If AI narrows the information asymmetry in mid- and large-cap global equities, the dispersion required for profitable short-selling and concentrated long positions diminishes.
3. **Democratisation of direct global investing.** Australian retail platforms (e.g., Stake, Interactive Brokers, Superhero) now allow investors to build custom global portfolios, including short positions via options and inverse products, reducing the need for an Australian-domiciled, ASX-quoted vehicle.

### Timeline & Probability Assessment
- **Passive/ETF threat:** Active now; high probability of continued market-share loss for high-fee active vehicles.
- **AI-driven research:** Medium-term (2–5 years); moderate probability. Fundamental long-short managers have historically adapted by focusing on illiquidity and behavioural edges, but GLS operates in the liquid developed-market universe where AI disruption is most likely.
- **Direct access platforms:** Active now; high probability of slowly cannibalising retail demand for global LICs.

### Company Response & Adaptation
There is no public evidence that GLS or L1 Capital is integrating quantitative or AI tools into its research process. The strategy remains explicitly fundamental, with co-CIO approval required for every position and a median long position trading at ~8x P/E as of March 2026 [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *. The firm appears to be betting on human-driven, contrarian stock selection rather than technological adaptation.

## 9. Bottom-Line Competitive & Macro Assessment

### Competitive Position Characterization
GLS occupies a **niche leadership position** as the only large-scale, pure global long-short LIC on the ASX, backed by one of Australia’s strongest alternative-equity brands. Its competitive reality is bifurcated: the strategy benefits from L1 Capital’s track record and a captive capital base, yet the vehicle itself is six months old, carries a high fee burden, and operates within a structurally declining Australian LIC sector that is losing share to ETFs.

### Key Competitive & Macro Drivers (confidence weighted)
1. **L1 Capital investment performance and brand durability** — *High confidence.* Near-term flows and NTA performance are overwhelmingly driven by the manager’s ability to generate alpha. The first-half NTA return of 9.2% and strategy inception returns above 50% annualised are the central demand drivers [Source: Quartr / Title: GLS H1 2026 Earnings Summary / Date: 2026-03-08] *.
2. **LIC sector discount/premium dynamics** — *High confidence.* GLS’s share price is anchored to NTA sentiment. The share price fell from ~A$1.90–1.98 in April 2026 to ~A$1.87 in early May 2026, while pre-tax NTA reportedly recovered toward ~A$1.97, compressing the premium to a slight discount [Source: Stockanalysis / Title: GLS Share Price & Overview / Date: 2026-05-07] *; [Source: Intelligent Investor / Title: GLS Announcements / Date: 2026-05-05] *.
3. **Macro volatility and cross-asset correlation** — *Medium confidence.* The Iran War demonstrated that geopolitical shocks can simultaneously crush long and short books (March 2026 return of –14.9%). Future volatility is a double-edged sword: it creates opportunity but can temporarily destroy alpha [Source: L1 Global Long Short Fund / Title: March 2026 Monthly Report / Date: 2026-03-31] *.
4. **Structural shift from active LICs to passive ETFs** — *High confidence.* This is a secular headwind not specific to GLS but shaping the entire addressable market.

### Key Competitive & Macro Risks (confidence weighted)
1. **Sustained widening of GLS discount to NTA** — *High confidence.* If the share price persistently decouples from underlying NTA—already observed in the March 2026 drawdown when the fund traded at a premium but faced –14.9% monthly performance—retail capital will flee and the captive-capital advantage reverses into a governance liability.
2. **Key-person departure at L1 Capital** — *Medium confidence.* The strategy is inseparable from Landau and Lamm. Their 95% ownership stake aligns interests but concentrates operational risk [Source: Platinum Asset Management / Title: ASX Presentation / Date: 2025-07-08] ***.
3. **Fee-based competitive erosion from passive and low-cost active** — *High confidence.* GLS must consistently generate 400–600 basis points of net alpha to justify its fee load against MFF and global ETFs. Failure to do so will result in structural outflows over the 1–2 year horizon.

### Catalysts to Watch
- **Monthly NTA announcements:** Next release expected early June 2026; will reveal whether April’s apparent NTA rebound (pre-tax ~A$1.97, post-tax ~A$1.865) is sustained or eroded by ongoing volatility [Source: Intelligent Investor / Title: GLS Announcements / Date: 2026-05-05] *.
- **FY2026 full-year results:** Expected August 2026; first full annual report under L1 management will include audited fee realisation and tax-basis NTA reconciliation.
- **RBA and Federal Reserve policy decisions:** Further hawkish pivots in response to energy-driven inflation would pressure leveraged gross exposure and short-book carry.
- **Iran War resolution or escalation:** A sustained closure of the Strait of Hormuz would maintain energy volatility and central bank hawkishness, directly affecting portfolio positioning.

## 10. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Exact April 2026 NTA figures:** The Monthly NTA announcement dated 5 May 2026 was referenced in third-party data feeds, but the underlying ASX PDF was not accessible. The pre-tax and post-tax figures of ~A$1.97 and ~A$1.865 were inferred from announcement metadata on Intelligent Investor. *Search scope: ASX announcements portal, Market Index, Intelligent Investor.*
- **Post-March 2026 portfolio-level performance:** Only the March 2026 monthly return (–14.9%) and early-April share price data are available. April strategy performance was not located in the search scope. *Search scope: L1 Capital website, Quartr, ASX announcements.*
- **Detailed short-book and prime broker identities:** The March 2026 report provided geographic and sector exposures but did not name individual short positions or prime brokerage counterparties. *Search scope: L1 Capital monthly reports, ASX filings.*

- **Reliance on simulated/forward-dated future data:** Several sources (e.g., March 2026 Iran War, 2026 company reports) reflect a simulated timeline. The analytical framework treats these as factual per the research date (2026-05-07), but the inherent uncertainty of geopolitical and market events is higher than for historically settled periods.

# Bull Case: L1 Global Long Short Fund Limited (GLS)

## Thesis Statement

GLS represents a rare convergence of top-quartile investment manager skill, a structurally advantaged closed-end vehicle, and a portfolio trading at cyclically depressed valuations following an exogenous geopolitical shock. The market is under-appreciating the durability of L1 Capital’s alpha generation and the structural resilience of the LIC format, which together allow the fund to exploit volatility rather than suffer from it. As the portfolio’s median long positions—already trading at single-digit earnings multiples—reprice and a 12-month fee holiday amplifies net returns, GLS is positioned to compound NTA per share at a rate that justifies its scarcity premium and delivers superior total returns.

## Thesis Pillars

### Pillar 1: Proven Manager Alpha with a Rapid Post-Shock Recovery Trajectory

**Claim:** L1 Capital has generated exceptional risk-adjusted alpha since the strategy’s inception, and the March 2026 drawdown was an exogenous geopolitical dislocation that the portfolio is already recovering from, not evidence of impaired strategy capacity.

**Argument:** The Global Long Short Strategy delivered a 69.6% net return in calendar 2025 and 53.9% annualised since January 2025, outperforming the MSCI World Accumulation Index by roughly 40 percentage points annualised. This performance was broad-based, with 51 individual positions each contributing more than 1.0% to returns, indicating deep stock-selection capability rather than a few lucky bets. When the Iran War triggered the worst oil supply shock in history and a global equity selloff in March 2026, the fund suffered a sharp but explainable drawdown of 14.9% in a month where 84% of S&P 500 stocks declined. Rather than freezing, the manager actively redeployed into gold, copper, European banks, and construction materials while trimming energy. The result was a rapid NTA rebound: post-tax NTA per share recovered from $1.55 on 31 March to $1.6404 by 30 April 2026. This V-shaped recovery suggests the underlying portfolio intrinsic value was never meaningfully impaired. The confidence of the manager and its founders is further evidenced by the roughly $162 million in cornerstone commitments—including approximately $90 million from L1 Group itself—during the transition. With co-CIOs Mark Landau and Raphael Lamm requiring joint approval on every position and maintaining a 95% ownership stake in the manager, the incentive and governance structure is ruthlessly aligned with long-term NTA compounding.

**Evidence Strength:** Strong

**Pillar Conviction:** High

---

### Pillar 2: The Captive-Capital Structure Transforms Volatility from a Liability into a Compounding Asset

**Claim:** GLS’s closed-end LIC structure eliminates redemption risk and empowers the manager to maintain high-conviction leveraged exposure through volatility, turning market dislocations into compounding opportunities rather than liquidity crises.

**Argument:** Traditional open-ended long-short funds face a fatal structural flaw: redemptions spike precisely when volatility rises, forcing fire-sales of longs and panicked covering of shorts at the worst possible prices. GLS has no redemption mechanism. Shareholders seeking liquidity must sell to other buyers on the ASX, leaving the portfolio’s capital base entirely stable. This captive-capital feature allowed L1 Capital to enter the March 2026 crisis with gross exposure at 335% of NAV—217% long and 118% short—and to maintain that exposure while actively adding to dislocated names. The fund held $458 million in portfolio cash deposits and $11.6 million cash at bank as of December 2025, providing a robust liquidity buffer to service prime broker arrangements without distress. This structural stability is not merely defensive; it is offensive. It means the manager can exploit the very volatility that damages weaker-structured competitors, buying fundamentally sound businesses at temporarily depressed prices and holding them through recovery. For investors, this transforms the LIC wrapper from a mere convenience into a genuine alpha-enhancing mechanism.

**Evidence Strength:** Strong

**Pillar Conviction:** High

---

### Pillar 3: Deep-Value Portfolio with Embedded Fee and Income Tailwinds

**Claim:** The current portfolio is concentrated in exceptionally cheap, high-quality global equities trading at single-digit multiples with strong earnings growth, while a 12-month management fee waiver and a $167.5 million Dividend Profit Reserve provide immediate, quantifiable tailwinds to reported net returns and distributions.

**Argument:** Following the Iran War selloff, the manager reported that the median long position in the GLS portfolio traded on approximately 8x P/E, offered double-digit EPS growth, and carried modest debt. These are not speculative growth stories at stretched multiples; they are established, cash-generative businesses acquired during a fear-driven liquidation. As geopolitical uncertainty recedes, the re-rating potential for this book is substantial. Compounding the fundamental upside, GLS currently benefits from a management fee waiver until 28 November 2026, eliminating a 1.44% annual cost drag that would otherwise accrue to the manager. This fee holiday acts as a direct, mechanical boost to net asset value accumulation over the coming months. Finally, the Board has ring-fenced a Dividend Profit Reserve of $167.5 million as at 31 December 2025, underpinning its stated intention to deliver a sustainable and growing stream of fully franked dividends. This reserve provides both a tangible floor for income and a signal that the fund’s accounting profits are being stewarded for shareholder distribution rather than absorbed by the corporate structure. Together, deep value in the underlying equities, fee relief, and distributable reserves create an unusually asymmetric setup where multiple levers can drive NTA and total shareholder returns simultaneously.

**Evidence Strength:** Moderate (The portfolio valuation metrics are sourced from manager commentary; the fee holiday and dividend reserve are documented in statutory disclosures.)

**Pillar Conviction:** High (The underlying logic is robust: buying cheap, high-quality assets during a crisis, amplified by mechanical cost and distribution tailwinds.)

## Synthesis

These three pillars form a coherent, mutually reinforcing thesis for superior compounding. Pillar 1 establishes that the engine works: L1 Capital has demonstrated an ability to generate broad-based, benchmark-crushing alpha in global equities. Pillar 2 explains why that alpha can survive the very volatility that destroys open-ended competitors: the LIC structure removes redemption-driven liquidity risk and allows the manager to lean into dislocation with maintained leverage. Pillar 3 provides the immediate trigger: the portfolio is currently invested in a collection of deeply undervalued, fundamentally sound businesses trading at single-digit multiples, while a fee waiver and dividend reserve provide mechanical return tailwinds. The unifying logic is that GLS is not merely a bet on a skilled manager; it is a bet on a skilled manager operating inside a structurally superior vehicle at a moment of maximum pessimism in the underlying portfolio. As the extreme fear that caused the March 2026 drawdown dissipates, the combination of NTA recovery, fee-enhanced compounding, and persistent scarcity value as the ASX’s only large-scale global long-short LIC should drive superior total returns.

## Overall Thesis Conviction

High
